lesson.title: Mean Reversion & Stationarity
lesson.body: A mean-reverting series is stationary — it oscillates around a long-run mean and deviations decay over time. The Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process dX = θ(μ−X)dt + σdW is a continuous-time mean-reverting model with mean reversion speed θ. The half-life of mean reversion is ln(2)/θ. The ADF test checks whether a financial series is mean-reverting.

rooms.question: A stock price time series that mean-reverts is:
answers: Stationary — it oscillates around a fixed level
rooms.explanation: Mean reversion implies stationarity: the series tends to return to its mean. Most raw stock prices are non-stationary; some spreads are mean-reverting.

rooms.question: In the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process dX = θ(μ−X)dt + σdW, larger θ means:
rooms.explanation: θ is the speed of mean reversion. Half-life = ln(2)/θ. Large θ → fast reversion → shorter half-life → more trading opportunities.

rooms.question: The half-life of mean reversion tells traders:
answers: How long it typically takes to revert halfway to the mean — sets the trade holding period
The maximum profit from the trade
The Sharpe ratio of the strategy
rooms.explanation: Half-life ≈ ln(2)/θ. A half-life of 5 days suggests positions should be held about 5 days and definitely closed before 3-4 half-lives.

rooms.question: Order the steps to test whether a spread is mean-reverting:
items: Run ADF test on the spread series
Check ADF p-value (< 0.05 → reject unit root → stationary)
Assess tradability: is half-life short enough?
rooms.explanation: ADF test → stationarity verdict → estimate half-life from φ̂ in Δy_t = φy_{t-1} + ε_t → assess if strategy is practical.

rooms.question: The Hurst exponent H < 0.5 indicates:
rooms.explanation: H < 0.5: mean-reverting (anti-persistent). H = 0.5: random walk. H > 0.5: trending (persistent, long memory).

boss.name: The Trending Market
boss.taunt: You expect a return to the mean. But what if I never return?

questions.question: The ADF test for mean reversion regresses Δy_t = α + βy_{t-1} + ε_t. H₀ is rejected (mean-reverting) when:
answers: β < 0 significantly (t-statistic below ADF critical value)
questions.explanation: ADF H₀: β=0 (random walk). We want to reject H₀, finding β < 0 (the series gets pulled back to the mean).

questions.question: If the ADF test for a series fails to reject the unit root (p=0.4), a trader should:
answers: Not trade this series as a mean-reversion strategy
Double check with Hurst exponent only
Proceed — p-value isn't relevant for trading
questions.explanation: Failing to reject a unit root means the spread may be a random walk, not mean-reverting. Trading mean-reversion on a random walk will lose money over time.

questions.question: An O-U process with half-life of 120 trading days is generally:
answers: Too slow for most systematic traders (6 months holding required)
questions.explanation: A 120-day half-life requires holding positions for months. Capital is tied up, transaction costs accumulate, and the strategy has few signal-to-noise cycles per year.
